beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. OpenAI announced a mega-app merging ChatGPT Work and Codex, plus a 5.6 model with Sol, Terra, and Luna tiers. ChatGPT Work spawns parallel agents like Claude Cowork, but its selector exposes thirty confusing tier, effort, and speed combinations. The practical rule: Sol-Ultra for planning, Luna-High for execution, and skip the middle Terra tier entirely. A shared usage cap can still interrupt work mid-task, which is a design problem, not an edge case.

artifactLines: OpenAI announced a mega-app merging ChatGPT Work and Codex, plus a 5.6 model with Sol, Terra, and Luna tiers.
ChatGPT Work spawns parallel agents like Claude Cowork, but its selector exposes thirty confusing tier, effort, and speed combinations.
The practical rule: Sol-Ultra for planning, Luna-High for execution, and skip the middle Terra tier entirely.
A shared usage cap can still interrupt work mid-task, which is a design problem, not an edge case.

beats.narration_text: The thirty-combination model selector sounds like exactly the kind of thing that quietly wastes my time. Before I trust ChatGPT Work for a real project, I want to stress-test the usage-cap trap. Help me (1) map when Sol-Ultra planning is actually worth it versus just defaulting to Luna-High execution, (2) design a single big task that would reveal whether the shared allowance runs out mid-work, and (3) set up a fair head-to-head of Sol-5.6 against Fable-5 on my own writing.

props.command: The thirty-combination model selector sounds like exactly the kind of thing that quietly wastes my time. Before I trust ChatGPT Work for a real project, I want to stress-test the usage-cap trap. Help me (1) map when Sol-Ultra planning is actually worth it versus just defaulting to Luna-High execution, (2) design a single big task that would reveal whether the shared allowance runs out mid-work, and (3) set up a fair head-to-head of Sol-5.6 against Fable-5 on my own writing.

beats.narration_text: Here's what OpenAI actually announced. A mega-app combining ChatGPT Work and Codex inside the ChatGPT interface. A new flagship model, 5.6, with three tiers: Sol at the top, Terra in the middle, Luna at the cheap end. ChatGPT Work is the non-code version — it spawns parallel agents to answer queries, just like Claude Cowork. Codex is the developer path. Five billion monthly visits. ChatGPT is the fifth most visited site on earth. Claude is thirty-seventh. Traffic-wise, this is not a fair fight.

artifactLines: ChatGPT Work: multi-agent mode, like Cowork — spawns parallel agents.
ChatGPT Codex: same engine, developer interface.
Model 5.6 in three tiers: Sol (expensive/smart), Terra (middle), Luna (cheap).
ChatGPT: 5th most visited site. Claude: 37th. Traffic gap is real.

beats.narration_text: But ChatGPT Work's interface is a disaster. Its model selector exposes thirty combinations — model tier, effort level, and speed setting, all stacked on top of each other. Luna-High, Terra-Ultra, Sol-Ultra, Light effort, Extra High effort. The practical rule: ignore most of it. Use Sol-Ultra for planning at the start of a big task. Use Luna-High for the actual execution. Skip Terra-Ultra — the middle tier is hard to justify. ChatGPT's design forces you to make decisions that should just be defaults.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B02.png (screenshot of the ChatGPT Work model/effort selector showing the confusing matrix of options)

beats.narration_text: A real end-to-end test exposes the usage-cap problem. During a planning-and-execution session, ChatGPT Work can hit this wall: 'You're out of Codex and Work usage.' Plan one task with Sol-Ultra, switch to the cheaper Luna-High tier for execution, and the shared allowance can still run out mid-task. The work itself was interrupted. Other users reported the same thing on X. This is not a usage edge case. It's a design problem.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B03.png (screenshot of the 'You're out of Codex and Work usage' error message in ChatGPT Work)

beats.narration_text: The real comparison is Sol-5.6 against Claude Fable-5. Sol is OpenAI's most powerful model in the new lineup. Fable-5 is currently the top-ranked AI model on earth by benchmarks — better than everything on coding, reasoning, and long-form work. A useful framing is a senior lawyer versus an intern: Fable-5 is the $1,000-an-hour partner you use for strategy. Sonnet-5 is the capable junior who executes. The problem is the same on both sides: if you use the expensive model for everything, the bill gets out of hand fast.

props.artifactHeading: How to think about the top models
artifactLines: Fable-5: best model by benchmarks. Use for planning, strategy, hard reasoning.
Sonnet-5 / Luna-High: the execution tier. Follows Fable's plan reliably.
Both ecosystems have the same problem: top tier is expensive, easy to overuse.
The senior-lawyer rule applies everywhere: match model to task complexity.

beats.narration_text: So does ChatGPT still matter? The answer is yes — conditionally. If your company already pays for ChatGPT Enterprise, use it properly. If you write most of your work in English and need long multi-step tasks done reliably, Claude Cowork is still the cleaner tool. If you need images, Google Sheets integration, or web search with citations, the new ChatGPT has pulled ahead. This is not a one-AI world. The honest stack is: Claude for deep work, ChatGPT for images and sheets, Gemini for non-English.

artifactLines: Claude Cowork: deep writing, long tasks, document work — still the best.
ChatGPT Work: images, Google Sheets, web search — genuinely better now.
Gemini: non-English work. Grok/Perplexity: research and news.
Usage cap on ChatGPT Work is a real problem. Plan accordingly.

beats.narration_text: The deeper issue isn't which model is smarter. Both companies push billing complexity onto the user, but in different ways. ChatGPT Work exposes thirty selector combinations and three usage pools that can run out independently. Its interface is a maze. Claude Cowork is cleaner to operate, but Claude is not innocent: Fable-5 left subscriptions after July 12th and now runs on usage billing. ChatGPT makes model selection confusing; Claude makes premium-model spending easy to miss. If you're running a team on either platform and nobody tracks spend, expect a large invoice.

props.artifactHeading: What to watch on both sides
artifactLines: ChatGPT Work: three separate usage pools — Work, Codex, and model tier.
Claude Fable-5: moved to usage billing July 12th. Not in subscription anymore.
Team risk: no one tracks spend, everyone uses the top model by default.
Set usage limits before you roll out either platform to a team.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Before you try ChatGPT Work or compare it to Cowork, run this prompt in Claude Cowork first. It gives you a structured comparison against your actual workflow — not a benchmark, your work.

props.command: I want to compare Claude Cowork and ChatGPT Work for my actual job. Interview me about my 3 most-repeated weekly tasks, then tell me which platform handles each one better and why — be honest about trade-offs.

props.title: ChatGPT shamelessly copied Claude.
props.subline: Cowork still leads on deep work
